title: The Price of Freedom Denied: Religious Persecution and Conflict in the 21st Century By Brian J. Grim and Roger Finke Cambridge University Press. 2010. 272 pages. $115 cloth, $39.95 paper
abstract: Working from the religious economies perspective, The Price of Freedom Denied argues that the most important predictor of religious persecution and violent conflict is not the “clash of civilizations” or extremist religious ideas, but is instead the extent to which governments and social forces deny religious freedoms, fail to protect religious minorities, and/or attempt to legally establish a single religion. The argument is based on coded data from the International Religious Freedom Reports produced by the U.S. State Department. The book's primary contribution is to empirically demonstrate, using cross-national data, a relationship between increased state restriction on religion and increased religious conflict. The authors' conclusions have different implications for different contexts but are broadly convincing and important. If a government aims to reduce religious violence and conflict, the best law and policy prescription is firm non-­establishment and unwavering protection of religious minorities. More religious freedoms lead to less persecution and violence; more restrictions or fewer protections lead to more persecution and violence.
